name: Fallback Authentication Architecture Selection
consequences: Immediate: Choosing between mobile-centric (GrapheneOS + FIDO2), browser-based (WebAuthn), or hardware-token approaches shapes which dependency classes can be eliminated → Systemic: A hardware-token-first strategy removes mobile OS coupling entirely but shifts deployment friction to ~15–20% higher citizen friction and delays AltID integration timelines by 6–12 months → Strategic: The choice determines whether the fallback serves only contingency (narrow constituency) or becomes a primary accessibility pathway (broader political traction and procurement leverage).
options: Design the demonstrator around a privacy-respecting Android fork (GrapheneOS) with FIDO2/OpenID Connect, accepting that it remains Android-dependent but proves open-stack viability within the current duopoly platform landscape.

Build a dual-track demonstrator combining browser-based WebAuthn for desktop/kiosk access with a FIDO2 hardware token (e.g., YubiKey-compatible) to eliminate mobile OS dependence entirely, accepting increased user-education burden and higher per-citizen deployment cost (~250–400 DKK per token).

Pursue a hardware-centric model with a Danish-designed or sovereign-hardware token that integrates with standard OpenID Connect and certified European identity providers, positioning the fallback as a long-term sovereignty investment rather than a short-term convenience play, accepting 18–24 month development and certification lag.
review: Controls technical feasibility vs. political scalability. Weakness: The options fail to consider interoperability with existing MitID-certified service providers and the regulatory pathway required to certify new authentication methods under NSIS Substantial level without full MitID integration.

name: Institutional Engagement and Gating Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Engaging Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, and AltID leadership in phase one creates formal veto points and relationship dependencies → Systemic: Early institutional capture of at least one decision-maker can shift ~30–40% of project risk from external advocacy to internal policy leverage, but also locks the project into a 6–9 month institutional feedback loop that delays independent publication and coalition-building → Strategic: Late or weak institutional engagement preserves coalition independence but reduces likelihood of inclusion in official procurement language by month 24 and increases reliance on EU standards pressure as the primary lever.
options: Establish formal advisory relationships with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and AltID leadership in month 2, framing the project as resilience support for their procurement process, accepting institutional constraints on messaging and the risk that unfavorable findings will be suppressed or delayed.

Conduct institutional consultations only after publishing the phase-one feasibility report independently, preserving advocacy credibility and coalition autonomy, accepting 3–4 month delay in institutional feedback and reduced likelihood of direct inclusion in AltID design cycles.

Pursue a dual-track approach: formal engagement with sympathetic Digitaliseringsstyrelsen technical teams in parallel with independent publication and EU standards-body engagement, balancing institutional access against message independence, accepting organizational overhead and the risk of conflicting institutional signals.
review: Controls autonomy vs. institutional influence. Weakness: The options fail to address the risk that the MitID operator will formally object to fallback-authentication research or classify demonstrator work as competitive threat, and the project has no explicit strategy for managing or escalating such opposition.

name: Coalition Scope and Messaging Frame
consequences: Immediate: Recruiting civil-society, academic, and privacy advocates positions the project as a public-interest initiative rather than a vendor or state actor → Systemic: A coalition spanning 8–12 credible organizations can amplify policy pressure by ~60% and provide institutional cover against dismissal as fringe or ideological, but also introduces messaging fragmentation and the risk that coalition partners prioritize privacy or anti-monopoly arguments over resilience framing, diluting the procurement-focused message by 25–35% → Strategic: A tightly focused coalition (4–5 organizations aligned on resilience framing) maintains message discipline and procurement credibility but lacks the grassroots and media amplification needed to sustain political pressure beyond month 18.
options: Build a broad coalition spanning digital-rights NGOs (e.g., Digital Rights Denmark, Forbrugernes Råd), academic security researchers, bank-sector contingency planners, and Folketinget green-party representatives, accepting messaging fragmentation and the need for extensive coordination overhead.

Form a narrowly focused technical coalition around publicly funded research institutions and digital-sovereignty-aligned policy specialists, maintaining coherent procurement-focused messaging, accepting reduced grassroots advocacy reach and vulnerability to dismissal as an insider-only initiative.

Launch a tiered coalition with an inner core (3 technical institutions + Digitaliseringsstyrelsen sympathizer) coordinating procurement language and a broader outer ring (privacy groups, consumer advocates, EU digital-rights networks) amplifying resilience messaging independently, accepting complexity in coalition governance but preserving both technical credibility and political reach.
review: Controls message focus vs. political reach. Weakness: The options fail to account for the risk that existing technology industry lobbies (especially incumbent identity-services vendors and platform integrators) will actively counter-message with cost and convenience arguments, and the project has no explicit budget or strategy for paid media or counter-narrative.

name: EU Standards and Interoperability Engagement
consequences: Immediate: Engaging European Digital Identity Wallet (EDIDW) working groups and EU standards bodies in phase two creates a channel for platform-neutral language insertion at the EU level → Systemic: A successful EU standards contribution can establish ~40–50% stronger precedent for platform-neutrality requirements across member states, but requires 4–6 month standards-body feedback cycles and the risk that EU working groups deprioritize fallback mechanisms in favor of interoperability (which may assume continued platform duopoly) → Strategic: Strong EU-level traction creates a compliance justification for Danish procurement requirements, but also dilutes the project from a uniquely Danish sovereignty initiative to a general-purpose interoperability effort, reducing political salience domestically.
options: Prioritize EU standards engagement early (by month 12) as a primary lever, aiming to insert platform-neutrality language into official EDIDW materials, accepting that this shifts project focus away from Danish procurement and toward slower EU consensus-building cycles.

Treat EU engagement as a secondary amplification channel used only to reinforce Danish procurement outcomes achieved through internal AltID influence, accepting that weak EU progress does not undermine the project but also limits broader European leverage.

Execute a dual-track EU strategy: support Nordic cross-border digital-identity working groups (involving Sweden and Estonia) to create peer-state pressure for platform neutrality, building regional consensus that is harder for Brussels or incumbents to dismiss, accepting 6–8 month additional timeline and the risk that other Nordic states deprioritize sovereignty framing.
review: Controls domestic vs. international leverage. Weakness: The options fail to consider that EU EDIDW processes are already advanced (as of 2026) and that inserting new platform-neutrality requirements mid-cycle may face formal procedural objections from the European Commission or incumbent wallet operators.

name: Demonstrator Scope and Production Readiness Boundary
consequences: Immediate: Defining a lean, non-production-certified demonstrator scope prevents scope creep and keeps budget within 4.0 million DKK for phase two → Systemic: A deliberately limited demonstrator (simulated IdP, no live MitID integration, GrapheneOS or equivalent) proves feasibility at ~40–50% lower cost but creates a credibility gap: stakeholders may dismiss it as 'not real' or too far from production readiness, requiring explicit reframing around contingency and architectural proof rather than replacement → Strategic: A boundary-respecting scope preserves coalition credibility and allows rapid iteration, but leaves the project vulnerable to 'but will it work at scale?' objections that may require expensive follow-on certification or production-integration work post-month 24.
options: Build a lightweight, simulated-environment demonstrator authenticating against a mock Danish public-sector IdP, explicitly positioning it as an architectural proof of concept and NOT a production alternative, accepting that early dismissals of the work as 'toy example' will require repeated clarification in advocacy work.

Develop a higher-fidelity demonstrator that integrates with one or more actual public-sector test environments (e.g., a sandbox MitID environment if access can be negotiated), trading scope and cost control for authenticity that reduces credibility gaps, accepting 8–12 month additional timeline and risk of institutional blockers.

Design a phased demonstrator starting with a simulated environment in phase two and deferring a production-grade fallback authentication path to a separate phase three+ effort funded separately, maintaining clear separation between proof-of-concept and production readiness, accepting fragmentation and the risk that phase three funding never materializes.
review: Controls cost and timeline vs. stakeholder credibility. Weakness: The options fail to address whether the demonstrator must meet NSIS Substantial level security requirements (which may impose certification costs of 500–800k DKK not currently budgeted) or whether a lower-assurance proof of concept is sufficient for procurement influence.

name: AltID Procurement Language and Regulatory Requirements Insertion
consequences: Immediate: Drafting specific procurement language requiring platform-neutral or certified fallback authentication paths creates a legal lever for including the project's recommendations in official AltID tender and design documents → Systemic: Inserting 2–3 procurement requirements (e.g., 'authentication shall not depend exclusively on proprietary app-store distribution' or 'fallback access path shall be certified to NSIS Substantial') increases probability of supplier-neutral design by ~35–45%, but also triggers legal review, incumbent vendor objections, and 4–6 month consensus-building within Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, delaying AltID design finalization by 6–9 months → Strategic: Hard procurement requirements lock in platform neutrality for the next 5–7 year identity cycle, but face strong incumbent resistance and may be watered down to non-binding 'should consider' language unless coalition and feasibility evidence are published simultaneously.
options: Draft prescriptive procurement language requiring certified fallback authentication by month 16 and lobby intensively for inclusion in the AltID RFP (target: month 20 RFP release), accepting that this front-loads institutional negotiation burden and risks rejection as too restrictive.

Propose softer guidance language (e.g., 'resilience considerations should include evaluation of platform-neutral access') for inclusion in AltID design briefs and vendor evaluation criteria, accepting that this preserves institutional goodwill and increases adoption likelihood, but reduces enforceability and allows vendors to dismiss platform-neutrality as non-mandatory.

Develop dual-track language: hard requirements for fallback authentication in a separate 'continuity and resilience' procurement stream, with platform-neutrality as a desirable criterion in the main AltID RFP, allowing vendors flexibility on primary path while ensuring fallback capability, accepting organizational complexity and risk of fragmentation between procurement streams.
review: Controls regulatory enforcement vs. incumbent acceptance. Weakness: The options fail to consider that AltID procurement decisions are likely already far advanced (as of month 8 of the project) and that inserting new requirements mid-cycle may be procedurally infeasible or require commissioning a formal delay.

name: Contingency Framing and Institutional Risk Narrative
consequences: Immediate: Reframing the issue from sovereignty ideology to concrete contingency and resilience (loss of app-store access, platform policy changes, geopolitical disruption) shifts receptiveness among conservative institutional audiences → Systemic: Contingency framing increases buy-in from risk-management and continuity-planning stakeholders by ~45–55% and reduces dismissal as 'anti-American' or anti-competitive, but also invites requests for quantified risk scenarios (e.g., 'how likely is Apple to deny MitID access in 2028?'), which are difficult to estimate credibly and may backfire if scenarios appear alarmist or speculative → Strategic: A well-executed contingency narrative becomes the primary justification for fallback-authentication requirements in procurement and policy, but requires explicit scenario development and stress-testing that consumes 150–200k DKK and 2–3 FTE-months in phase one.
options: Develop a formal risk and impact assessment quantifying scenarios: probability of app-store policy changes, estimated downtime/citizen impact if MitID mobile access is disrupted, and comparative risk of fallback-free vs. fallback-capable architectures, accepting that quantification may appear speculative and invites methodological criticism.

Frame contingency narratively, referencing historical precedents (e.g., Parler app removal, India's app bans, China's app restrictions) and institutional best practices from critical-infrastructure resilience (e.g., power-grid and banking failover requirements), accepting that narrative framing is less 'objective' but avoids false precision and maintains institutional flexibility.

Combine lightweight scenario development (3–5 plausible disruption scenarios with order-of-magnitude impact estimates) with qualitative resilience principles drawn from financial-sector and healthcare-sector continuity standards, balancing quantitative credibility with qualitative flexibility, accepting higher complexity in communication and the need for external facilitator to stress-test assumptions with skeptical audiences.
review: Controls risk credibility vs. alarmism. Weakness: The options fail to address whether contingency framing will be undercut by incumbent operators (Apple, Google, platform integrators) claiming that app-store policy disruptions are extremely unlikely and that contingency investment is wasteful.

name: Fallback Trust Model Anchor Selection
consequences: Immediate: Choosing between a distributed-trust model (multi-authority signing), a Danish state-hosted anchor, or a hybrid EU-backed trust infrastructure determines whether fallback authentication can be legally and operationally independent from current platform operators. Systemic: A distributed model increases implementation complexity by approximately 30–40% but reduces single-point-of-failure risk by an estimated 60%; a state-hosted model accelerates domestic deployment timelines by 4–6 months but creates political vulnerability if perceived as surveillance infrastructure. Strategic: The choice either enables fallback systems to survive geopolitical or platform-policy disruption, or perpetuates hidden dependency on whichever entity controls the anchor—defeating the sovereignty objective entirely.
options: Establish a dedicated Danish state trust anchor operated by the national digital authority with formal separation from MitID, requiring new legislation and dedicated operational funding but providing legal clarity and domestic accountability.

Co-design a multi-authority trust model with Sweden, Germany, and other EU members under eIDAS2 governance, distributing trust across national borders but requiring 12–18 months of alignment negotiation and shared infrastructure investment.

Delegate anchor trust to an existing quasi-independent technical body such as the Danish Internet Standards Board or a newly chartered not-for-profit consortium, reducing government friction but introducing organizational sustainability risk and potential legitimacy challenges during institutional crises.
review: Controls political risk (state authority vs. distributed governance) vs. operational velocity (rapid deployment vs. negotiated alignment). Weakness: The options do not address what happens to fallback systems if the chosen trust anchor is itself compromised, legally challenged, or subject to pressure from other EU member states.

name: Demonstrator Validation and External Credibility
consequences: Immediate: Choosing whether demonstrators are reviewed only by project-internal teams, by a contracted independent security firm, or by a formal EU or Danish regulatory body determines whether stakeholders treat them as proof-of-concept or propaganda. Systemic: Independent third-party security review adds 8–12 weeks and 150,000–250,000 DKK but increases institutional credibility by an estimated 70–80% and creates defensible audit trails for procurement discussions; internal-only validation preserves schedule but leaves demonstrators vulnerable to dismissal as self-serving or technically naive. Strategic: External validation either transforms demonstrators into credible technical evidence that influences AltID procurement decisions, or reduces them to internal R&D artifacts that procurement teams can safely ignore when facing convenience and incumbent-operator pressure.
options: Commission a formal independent security assessment from an ISO 27001–certified firm with Danish market presence and regulatory experience, building demonstrator credibility through formal certification but embedding review timelines and cost into the project budget.

Engage the Danish Defence and Security Administration or an equivalent state-mandated security authority to conduct demonstrator review as part of formal resilience-planning due diligence, securing government backing but accepting slower review cycles and potential classification restrictions.

Invite a consortium of external academic security researchers and EU-recognized authentication experts to conduct a published peer review, creating scholarly legitimacy and establishing open critique mechanisms but accepting that peer review does not guarantee procurement acceptance.
review: Controls credibility source (market-driven vs. state-backed vs. academic) vs. deployment speed (rapid vs. regulatory alignment). Weakness: The options assume that any form of external validation will shift procurement behavior, but do not account for institutional lock-in, where decision-makers may treat external validation as advisory rather than binding, particularly if incumbent operators lobby against demonstrator findings.

name: Supplier Concentration Risk Narrative Positioning
consequences: Immediate: Framing the fallback authentication need as a resilience and supplier-risk problem—rather than ideology or privacy maximalism—determines whether procurement stakeholders perceive it as a core continuity requirement or a nice-to-have alternative. Systemic: Risk-and-continuity framing increases estimated procurement receptiveness by 40–55% compared to pure privacy/sovereignty arguments, particularly among government CIOs and budget holders; it also activates existing corporate-risk-management mandates and audit requirements that already demand supplier diversification. Strategic: If risk framing takes institutional hold, platform-neutral requirements become enforceable through standard procurement governance rather than requiring special policy campaigns—but if incumbents successfully reframe the issue as technical overkill or government overreach, the project loses its structural advantage and must rely on political pressure alone.
options: Build detailed supplier-concentration risk indices showing single points of failure, app-store policy change scenarios, and estimated service-disruption costs, presenting them at national and EU continuity-planning forums and embedding risk language into AltID governance documents.

Commission a scenario-planning exercise from a respected consulting firm or academic think tank modeling geopolitical, commercial, and technical disruption scenarios, producing institutional white papers that procurement committees must formally address in their decision documentation.

Partner with Denmark's financial-sector regulators and large bank contingency planners to demonstrate how supplier concentration in digital identity creates systemic banking-sector risk, leveraging existing regulatory authority to mandate supplier-neutral authentication pathways alongside MitID.
review: Controls narrative ownership (internal framing vs. external authority vs. sector-specific risk) vs. institutional acceptance (grass-roots advocacy vs. formal risk governance). Weakness: The options do not address counter-narratives from incumbent operators, who will argue that their systems are already resilient, that distributed fallback systems introduce new risk, and that convenience and security certification are more important than supplier diversity.

name: AltID Integration Timing and Procurement Window Exploitation
consequences: Immediate: Determining whether the project directly engages AltID procurement teams before month 12, during phase-two demonstrator work, or holds engagement until phase three (post-month 16) affects whether platform-neutral requirements can be baked into the system specification versus applied as retrofit constraints later. Systemic: Early engagement (months 6–10) provides a 6-month window to influence architectural decisions and formal requirements, but risks early rejection if demonstrators are not yet credible; late engagement (months 18–24) ensures demonstrators are robust but compresses the window for procurement integration from 8 months to 4, reducing likelihood of formal inclusion by an estimated 35–45%. Strategic: Timing determines whether the project acts as a co-design partner in AltID's evolution or appears as an external critic attempting retrofit changes—affecting both political relationships and the probability that platform-neutral fallback mechanisms are institutionalized versus marginalized as optional future work.
options: Secure formal early-engagement status within AltID's governance and requirements-gathering process by month 6, using phase-one coalition and feasibility work to position the project as a risk-management contributor rather than an external pressure group, accepting high-touch relationship management burden.

Delay direct AltID engagement until phase-two demonstrators are substantially complete (month 15), preserving technical credibility and independent evidence before entering procurement discussions, but accepting that the window for influencing core architecture may have already closed.

Pursue parallel engagement through EU-level eIDAS2 implementation forums and national digital-strategy committees, building consensus and standards language at multiple governance levels simultaneously so that AltID faces pressure from above (EU standards) and below (national risk committees) rather than depending on direct project advocacy.
review: Controls engagement leverage (early partnership vs. late independent evidence vs. multi-level pressure) vs. credibility timing (demonstrators ready vs. untested vs. mature). Weakness: The options assume AltID decision-makers are open to external input, but do not account for the possibility that AltID procurement is already locked into platform-dependent design or that early engagement could trigger incumbent defensive moves that harden existing dependencies.

name: Hardware Token and Browser-Based Fallback Prioritization
consequences: Immediate: Choosing whether the phase-two secondary demonstrator emphasizes a dedicated hardware token (FIDO2 USB key or smart card), a browser-based authentication pathway (reducing OS dependency entirely), or a hybrid approach determines both implementation scope and the breadth of access scenarios the fallback pathway can address. Systemic: A dedicated token approach achieves approximately 85–90% technical independence from mobile OS but requires citizen adoption of new hardware, creating a behavioral barrier affecting an estimated 30–40% of users; a browser-based approach reduces hardware friction but leaves fallback authentication dependent on browser vendors (Google Chrome, Mozilla, Apple Safari) and thus does not fully escape the platform-dependency problem. Strategic: The choice determines whether fallback authentication becomes a practical, widely-usable contingency or remains a technically sound but adoption-limited escape hatch—affecting both resilience claims and the project's credibility in arguing that platform-neutral alternatives are genuinely operationally feasible for mass-market public-sector access.
options: Prioritize hardware-token implementations compatible with standard FIDO2 USB specifications and Danish public-sector IT infrastructure, accepting limited user adoption but ensuring technical sovereignty and compatibility with legacy authentication infrastructure in healthcare, tax, and banking systems.

Design a browser-based passwordless fallback pathway using WebAuthn and biometric prompts available across all major browsers, maximizing potential user reach and reducing hardware barriers but accepting residual dependence on browser-platform vendors and platform OS integrity for device binding.

Develop a hybrid approach combining a lightweight hardware token (NFC smart card or Bluetooth-based device) with a browser fallback option, allowing users to choose their security model and authentication channel, but doubling implementation complexity and testing burden to approximately 150% of single-channel effort.
review: Controls adoption barrier (hardware requirement vs. browser availability vs. user choice) vs. technical sovereignty (OS independence vs. browser dependency vs. layered alternatives). Weakness: The options do not address how fallback authentication pathways will integrate with existing legacy authentication systems in government agencies that may lack browser support or FIDO2 compatibility, potentially creating parallel authentication infrastructure rather than genuine platform-neutral convergence.

name: Coalition Sustainability and Competing-Interest Alignment
consequences: Immediate: Choosing whether the coalition is maintained as a loosely-coordinated stakeholder network, formally chartered as a legal entity with member commitments, or embedded within existing institutional structures (e.g., a parliamentary digital-policy working group) determines whether coalition members remain engaged through low-momentum periods and whether competing interests are managed through ongoing governance or informal relationships. Systemic: A formal legal entity increases coordination overhead by approximately 25–30% and requires bylaws and decision procedures, but creates accountability mechanisms and sustains engagement during phases when media attention drops; an informal network preserves flexibility but risks member defection if the project faces setbacks or if competing priorities claim attention, estimated 40–50% risk of coalition attrition during month 12–16 transition. Strategic: Coalition durability either enables sustained pressure on procurement, standards, and EU-level forums across the full 24-month timeline, or collapses after phase-one political momentum fades, leaving the project isolated when most institutional influence is needed.
options: Establish a formal not-for-profit coalition organization with published bylaws, regular governance meetings, and member-contribution expectations, requiring administrative overhead and legal startup costs (estimated 80,000–120,000 DKK) but creating institutional permanence and accountability beyond the 24-month project timeline.

Embed the coalition within an existing Danish digital-governance forum or parliamentary working group, leveraging existing institutional infrastructure and meeting cadences but accepting constraints on independent messaging and risk of member attention being diluted by competing parliamentary priorities.

Maintain a managed-but-informal coalition structure with quarterly member assemblies, published decisions and positions, and designated lead organizations responsible for specific workstreams, preserving flexibility and lower overhead but requiring constant relationship maintenance and explicit renewal of member commitments every 6 months.
review: Controls governance model (formal entity vs. institutional embedding vs. managed informality) vs. administrative burden (high structure vs. existing infrastructure vs. relationship-dependent). Weakness: The options do not address how coalition members with conflicting commercial interests (e.g., some vendors favoring platform-neutral requirements vs. others benefiting from current platform dependencies) will manage internal disagreement, or how the coalition will handle public disagreements that undermine unified messaging to procurement authorities.

name: Phase-Three Escalation and De-scoping Contingency Triggers
consequences: Immediate: Establishing explicit decision gates at month 16 (end of phase two) that determine whether phase three pursues direct AltID procurement engagement, shifts toward EU-standards influence, or deprioritizes Danish procurement impact in favor of consolidating research outputs and coalition legacy determines whether the project pivots opportunistically or commits to an increasingly difficult procurement campaign despite weak traction. Systemic: Clear escalation triggers (e.g., 'if AltID procurement teams have not formally acknowledged platform-neutral requirements by month 15, reduce procurement spend by 40% and reallocate to EU standards work') reduce estimated budget waste by 20–25% and prevent the project from chasing diminishing returns; conversely, ambiguous contingency criteria risk either premature abandonment of viable procurement pathways or stubborn pursuit of blocked strategies into month 24. Strategic: Robust contingency planning either preserves the project's strategic and intellectual capital by pivoting toward impact levers that are actually responsive (EU standards, academic publication, coalition permanence), or demoralizes the team and leaves 2–3 million DKK of phase-three budget committed to low-probability procurement outcomes rather than guaranteed deliverables.
options: Establish a formal month-16 go/no-go decision framework with quantified procurement-traction thresholds (e.g., formal written response from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, documented AltID architectural engagement, or inclusion of supplier-neutrality language in draft procurement documents), triggering automatic de-scope to EU standards and publication if thresholds are not met.

Create an adaptive phase-three budget with front-loaded procurement engagement and a pre-agreed reallocation clause: if procurement momentum has not materialized by month 14, remaining phase-three funds automatically redeploy toward European standards bodies, academic publications, and coalition institutionalization without requiring formal project amendment.

Develop a scenario-planning exercise during phase two that models four distinct month-16 outcomes (strong AltID engagement, weak engagement, incumbent resistance, or regulatory freeze), with pre-negotiated phase-three strategies and budget allocations for each scenario, enabling rapid pivoting without mid-project re-planning.
review: Controls contingency governance (formal thresholds vs. adaptive reallocation vs. scenario pre-planning) vs. strategic flexibility (hard gates vs. budgetary triggers vs. narrative adaptation). Weakness: The options assume that quantified thresholds and budget reallocation mechanisms will actually be respected during execution, but do not account for institutional inertia, where project teams and funding bodies often continue pursuing blocked procurement pathways regardless of contingency criteria because sunk costs and relationship investments create psychological pressure to persist.

name: Procurement Language Embedding and Contractual Binding Mechanism
consequences: Immediate: Draft platform-neutrality and fallback-access clauses into Danish procurement templates and AltID tender specifications before formal RFP release. Systemic: Early embedding reduces bidder ability to strip clauses during negotiation and increases likelihood that 60–70% of responding vendors pre-engineer solutions aligned with requirements rather than treating fallback as bolt-on afterthought. Strategic: Shifts the risk from post-award renegotiation (where incumbent operators have negotiating power) to pre-bid solution design, making supplier-neutral architectures competitively advantageous rather than costly exceptions.
options: Inject explicit 'platform-neutrality' and 'certified fallback mechanism' requirements directly into AltID tender specifications and procurement criteria at phase three entry, binding vendors to technical non-discrimination clauses before bid submission.

Propose a parallel procurement track for fallback authentication (run 12–18 months after main AltID tender) to give vendors time to adapt, reducing resistance but accepting later integration and higher de-scoping risk if fallback track stalls.

Work with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen to embed 'supplier resilience audits' into routine identity-infrastructure procurement, making platform-concentration analysis a standing evaluation criterion across all future digital-identity tenders independent of any single AltID project trigger.
review: Controls pushing early procurement lock-in (reduces negotiation later) vs. allowing vendor adaptation time (lowers resistance but delays leverage). Weakness: The options fail to consider how to enforce compliance if vendors satisfy language on paper but design fallback paths that are operationally fragile or prohibitively expensive to activate.

name: Demonstrator Credibility Sequencing and External Validation Gating
consequences: Immediate: Schedule phase two demonstrators to conclude 4–6 weeks before phase three procurement engagement intensifies, enabling third-party security audits and formal publication of results. Systemic: External validation from recognized security researchers or EU body reviewers increases institutional confidence by 35–45% (measured by formal written feedback or citation in procurement documents) compared to project self-attestation alone. Strategic: Positions Denmark as evidence-driven rather than ideologically motivated, deflecting incumbent operator arguments that fallback paths are academically interesting but operationally unproven.
options: Conduct independent security audits of both demonstrators by external accredited labs (e.g., EU digital-identity scheme reviewers or recognized cybersecurity consultancies) and publish audit reports as public policy evidence before month 18.

Invite live technical walkthroughs and hands-on validation sessions with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, MitID operator, and AltID program managers at the end of phase two, creating buy-in through direct observation rather than published reports.

Open demonstrator code and architecture to a time-limited public challenge period (4–8 weeks, month 15–16) inviting security researchers, vendors, and academics to stress-test implementations and publish findings, accepting reputational risk to gain community credibility.
review: Controls between rushed external review (weak validation, missed findings) vs. extended validation timelines (delays procurement engagement). Weakness: The options fail to consider how to manage demonstrator scope drift or security findings that undermine fallback viability late in phase two, forcing de-scoping of phase three procurement leverage.

name: EU Standards Alignment vs. Danish Autonomy Navigation
consequences: Immediate: Map phase three engagement with EU Digital Identity Wallet working groups and eIDAS regulatory bodies, identifying pressure points where Danish fallback and platform-neutrality language can align with or push European interoperability requirements. Systemic: Successful EU alignment (inclusion of fallback language in official EU wallet guidance or standards documents by month 20) multiplies Denmark's procurement leverage by 2–3x, as vendors globally anticipate EU-wide requirements; misalignment creates 'Denmark-only' cost premium of 8–12% that weakens domestic institutional support. Strategic: Either anchors Danish requirements to broader European sovereignty narratives, or risks isolating Danish procurement as an outlier that vendors can selectively deprioritize.
options: Tightly coordinate Danish phase three advocacy with EU Digital Identity Wallet standards bodies, ensuring that platform-neutrality language is jointly developed and that Danish requirements align verbatim with EU interoperability guidance, maximizing leverage but accepting that Danish autonomy is subordinated to EU consensus.

Develop Danish requirements independently in phases one and two, then present to EU bodies as 'Danish innovation case study' for European adoption in later standards cycles, allowing Danish autonomy but accepting 18–36 month lag before EU-level enforcement.

Engage selectively with EU working groups on specific technical topics (e.g., hardware-token fallback mechanisms, FIDO2 interoperability) without formally aligning procurement language, creating intellectual influence without institutional commitment and preserving Danish flexibility to diverge if needed.
review: Controls between EU-coordinated lock-in (stronger leverage, reduced autonomy) vs. Danish-first innovation (autonomy, slower adoption). Weakness: The options fail to consider how to manage conflicting guidance if EU and Danish authorities take different positions on fallback design or timeline, forcing the project to choose between institutional coherence and technical optimality.

name: Hardware-Token and Browser-Channel Fallback Positioning and Dependency Substitution
consequences: Immediate: Phase two devotes one demonstrator track to hardware-token or browser-based fallback mechanisms that reduce dependence on mobile-OS platform integrity signals. Systemic: Hardware-token or browser approaches shift trust anchor from Apple/Google APIs to FIDO2 certification, national smart-card infrastructure, or EU eIDAS trust lists, lowering platform lock-in cost by 45–60% (measured by reduction in permission-set coupling to OS vendor services). Strategic: Creates architectural optionality: even if mobile-OS fallback is incomplete, citizens retain access via alternative channels, improving resilience narrative and making supplier concentration less critical.
options: Prioritize hardware-token demonstrator (Danish national ID card with FIDO2 co-processor or separate hardware security key) on the assumption that citizens already carry identity credentials and that hardware-based trust is institutionally more durable than software-only approaches.

Develop browser-based fallback using OpenID Connect and FIDO2 WebAuthn on commodity browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Safari) without hardware requirement, reducing citizen friction and cost but accepting larger surface area for OS-level attack and weaker institutional narrative about 'true' platform independence.

Pursue dual-track demonstrator covering both hardware-token and browser approaches, explicitly showing architects that fallback is achievable across multiple technical modalities, but accepting higher phase-two budget pressure (estimated 15–20% increase) and risk of scope creep.
review: Controls between institutionally durable hardware anchoring vs. friction-free browser accessibility. Weakness: The options fail to consider how to resolve architectural conflicts if hardware-token and browser-based fallback paths disagree on trust models or regulatory compliance, creating downstream procurement confusion.

name: Coalition Stability and Competing-Agenda Harmonization
consequences: Immediate: Phase one coalition includes civil-society privacy advocates (focused on user control), contingency-planning stakeholders (focused on service continuity), academic security researchers (focused on technical rigor), and sympathetic lawmakers (focused on sovereignty narratives). Systemic: Coalition coherence degrades by 20–30% (measured by public statement alignment and meeting attendance) at month 12–14 as competing stakeholder priorities resurface; privacy advocates push for user-agency language, contingency planners demand network-resilience specs, security researchers publish findings that contradict simplifying narratives. Strategic: Weak coalition messaging in phase three allows incumbent operators to fragment support ('privacy advocates don't actually endorse this,' 'researchers say it's not ready'), reducing procurement pressure by 40–50%.
options: Establish coalition governance at month 3 with explicit decision-making rules, quarterly alignment sessions, and pre-approved messaging on core points (platform-neutrality, fallback access, resilience), requiring consensus before public statements and accepting slower coalition growth but reducing messaging drift.

Maintain loose coalition structure with minimal formal coordination, allowing stakeholders to advocate independently while project provides shared evidence base, maximizing coalition diversity but accepting public contradictions and tactical vulnerability to incumbent operator narrative fragmentation.

Create two-tier coalition with inner 'steering circle' (4–5 aligned organizations) that coordinates messaging and procurement engagement, and broader 'supporter network' (8–12 organizations) that endorses core principles without operational input, balancing coherence and autonomy but creating resentment among excluded stakeholders.
review: Controls between tight coordination (coherence, slow decision-making) vs. loose independence (agility, narrative fragmentation). Weakness: The options fail to consider how to manage situations where core coalition members publicly endorse competing procurement language, forcing the project to choose between organizational loyalty and technical clarity.

name: Phase-Three Escalation Triggering and De-scoping Contingency Activation
consequences: Immediate: Define month-16 decision gates assessing whether Digitaliseringsstyrelsen has formally engaged with AltID procurement planning and whether fallback and platform-neutrality language has been incorporated into planning documents. Systemic: If both gates pass, proceed with full phase three procurement-language development and vendor engagement; if both fail, shift phase three budget (estimated 60–70% of planned expenditure) toward publication, EU standards positioning, and coalition consolidation, reducing near-term procurement impact but preserving institutional capital and research output. Strategic: Contingency de-scoping prevents the project from spending 3.5 million DKK on phase three procurement engagement with disengaged or hostile institutions, and allows graceful pivot to standards and narrative influence that may mature more slowly but carry lower institutional-resistance risk.
options: Establish binding month-16 go/no-go criteria (formal Digitaliseringsstyrelsen engagement + incorporation of platform-neutrality language in planning documents) and automatically shift to EU-and-publication-focused phase three if both criteria are unmet, accepting reduced near-term procurement influence but eliminating wasted institutional effort.

Conduct month-16 assessment with flexibility to continue partial phase-three procurement engagement even if Digitaliseringsstyrelsen response is lukewarm, on the assumption that continued pressure and demonstrator publication may shift institutional position by month 20, accepting higher sunk-cost risk but preserving optionality for late procurement capture.

Pre-commit to phase-three de-scoping toward EU standards and coalition narrative work regardless of month-16 assessment outcomes, using the month-16 gate only for internal learning and not for go/no-go decisions, accepting that procurement influence is de-prioritized but eliminating institutional pivot anxiety.
review: Controls between crisp go/no-go discipline (clear decision-making, potential regret if conditions shift by month 17) vs. flexible contingency (resilience to late institutional movement, risk of extended unproductive engagement). Weakness: The options fail to consider how to define 'formal engagement' and 'incorporation of platform-neutrality language' in operational terms, creating ambiguity at the moment of greatest institutional pressure.

name: Incumbent Operator Accommodation and Co-Design Runway
consequences: Immediate: Approach MitID operator and Apple/Google partnerships in phase one with explicit request for technical dialogue on fallback scenarios and platform-stress conditions (rather than confrontational audit). Systemic: MitID operator willingness to co-design fallback mechanisms (estimated 20–30% probability) creates 'managed transition' narrative that reduces political friction and increases AltID institutional support by 35–45%; refusal creates incumbent-resistance narrative that strengthens coalition and procurement-language case but triggers direct operator lobbying against phase three. Strategic: Early inclusive dialogue either converts MitID operator into co-designer (strengthening technical and institutional credibility) or crystallizes their opposition (clarifying the resilience problem and reducing ambiguity about institutional pressure needed).
options: Invite MitID operator and platform providers to co-author fallback and platform-neutrality scenarios as part of phase one research, framing the exercise as joint resilience planning rather than competitive threat, accepting risk that operator input delays or dilutes fallback concepts but building institutional goodwill and credibility.

Conduct phase one research independently and present findings to MitID operator only at month 6–7 as a completed feasibility report, avoiding co-design but preserving research autonomy and preventing operator veto of demonstrator scope, accepting later operator resistance during phase three.

Run parallel workstreams: public-facing phase one research independent of operator input, plus a confidential 'technical advisory dialogue' with MitID operator exploring fallback scenarios off-record, allowing flexibility to incorporate operator feedback into phase two if dialogue is constructive or to publicly separate from operator positions if dialogue stalls.
review: Controls between early inclusive engagement (risk of dilution, potential for co-design) vs. independent research (autonomy, later institutional friction). Weakness: The options fail to consider how to manage situations where the MitID operator provides substantive technical input during phase one but refuses to publicly endorse it in phase three, creating ambiguity about technical credibility and forcing the project to decide whether to credit or disown operator contributions.
